mnemonics.title: Hamming Code Parity Bit Positions
mnemonics.content: Remember: P1 checks bits 1,3,5,7,9,11... (positions with LSB=1); P2 checks bits 2,3,6,7,10,11... (positions with 2nd bit=1); P4 checks bits 4-7,12-15... (positions with 3rd bit=1). Think: 'P1Odd, P2Two, P4Four' - position numbers match their parity bit.

mnemonics.title: Go-Back-N vs Selective Repeat
mnemonics.content: GBN: 'Go Back' means go back and resend ALL after error. SR: 'Selective' means select ONLY the error frame to resend.

acronyms.usage: Protocol that automatically retransmits corrupted or lost frames
Control message sent by receiver to confirm successful receipt
Control message requesting retransmission of specific frame
Measure of the number of bit errors per unit time
Error detection field in data link layer frames

visualTips.visualization: Think of a conveyor belt: the window slides forward as ACKs arrive, freeing new slots for sending frames. Frames inside the window are 'in-flight' (sent but not acknowledged).
Imagine a row of light bulbs where a section goes dark - that's a burst error affecting consecutive bits. Single-bit error is like just one bulb flickering.
Picture counting people in a room: even parity means even count of '1s'. If you enter/leave alone (1 bit flips), count changes from even to odd - error detected!
Like a movie director yelling 'CUT' and making the entire cast redo from scene of mistake, not just the actor who flubbed.

analogies.analogy: Like sending letters by certified mail: you send one letter, wait for delivery confirmation before sending the next. If confirmation doesn't arrive, you resend the same letter.
Like a secret handshake between sender and receiver. They both know a special 'dance move' (generator polynomial). If the received 'dance' doesn't match, they know something went wrong.
Like a bus with limited seats. The window size is the number of empty seats. As passengers (frames) board (get ACKs), seats open up, allowing new passengers to board.
The minimum number of character changes needed to transform one word into another. For error detection, codewords must differ in enough positions to detect errors.
